plan.description:
The Data Management Plan (DMP) describes how research data generated within the SPERMFREE project will be collected, processed, stored, protected, and preserved throughout the project lifecycle. The project will generate several types of data, including next-generation sequencing (NGS) data from seminal plasma cell-free DNA, derived bioinformatic datasets (e.g., fragmentomic profiles, microbial signatures, and inferred gene-expression features), and associated pseudonymized clinical metadata.

Raw sequencing data and intermediate computational files will be processed and temporarily stored using the High-Performance Computing (HPC) infrastructure provided by the Riga Technical University (RTU) High-Performance Computing Center. This infrastructure supports large-scale genomic data processing and secure storage required for next-generation sequencing analyses.

Curated datasets supporting scientific analyses and publications will be archived in the institutional RSU Dataverse repository to ensure long-term preservation and compliance with FAIR data principles. Sensitive patient-related information will remain pseudonymized and stored in secure institutional systems.

The bioinformatic workflow developed in this project constitutes a potentially patentable analytical technology; therefore, the pipeline source code will be maintained in a private version-controlled repository accessible only to the project team. Public release of proprietary components will be considered after completion of intellectual property protection procedures.
plan.title: SPERMFREE: integrated analytical workflow for cell-free DNA (cfDNA)-based assessment of testicular function in severe male infertility

systemField.label: Description
systemField.description: Briefly describe the context and purpose of the DMP

systemField.label: Language
systemField.description: Select the language of your DMP

referenceField.label: Researchers
referenceField.description: Add here the names of people that have produced, processed, analysed the data described in the DMP.

referenceField.label: Organizations
referenceField.description: Add here the names of the organizations contributing to the creation and revision of the DMPs

referenceField.label: Funding organizations
referenceField.description: Select a funder of your research or add new

referenceField.label: Grants
referenceField.description: Find the grant of your research or add new

referenceField.label: Project
referenceField.description: Projects in Argos are perceived as distinct activities falling under a grant or common activities under different grants in collaborative schemas, eg open call for contributions. Please complete it for the grant associated to your organization if your project falls under this category. In all other cases, please leave blank and it will be autocompleted.

systemField.label: Access Rights
systemField.description: Choose how the DMP is displayed after is published on Zenodo. By choosing Open Access, the DMP will be open on Zenodo after the Publication Date. By choosing Restricted Access, the DMP will be restricted after the publication is made.

referenceField.label: License
referenceField.description: Assign a license to your DMP by selecting the most appropriate from the list.

description.description: The following section outlines the research data management strategy for the SPERMFREE project. It describes the types of data generated during the project, including next-generation sequencing data from seminal plasma cell-free DNA, derived bioinformatic datasets, and associated pseudonymized clinical metadata. The plan defines how these data will be processed, stored, protected, and preserved throughout the project lifecycle using institutional research infrastructures, including the RTU High Performance Computing (HPC) environment for large-scale genomic data processing and the RSU Dataverse repository for long-term archiving of curated datasets. The approach ensures compliance with FAIR data principles while protecting sensitive patient information and intellectual property related to the analytical workflow developed in the project.
description.label: SPERMFREE: integrated analytical workflow for cell-free DNA (cfDNA)-based assessment of testicular function in severe male infertility

descriptionTemplate.label: RSU DMP Template
descriptionTemplate.description: This is the data managament plan for Riga Stradins University researchers

fieldSet.title: What is the purpose of the data collection/generation?
fieldSet.description: Give short description of the purpose of the data collection/generation and its relation to the objectives of the project. Also give short summary of the origin of the data

fieldSet.title: To whom the data might be useful ("data utility")?
fieldSet.description: Describe potential users of this dataset in the future. For example, researchers or students of your university or other research organisations. Research data can be useful also to governmental and non-governmental organisations working in the field, as well as to private sector.

section.description: In this section you state the purpose of the data collection/generation, as well as explain the relation to the objectives of the project. Give a summary of the origin of the data. Outline the data utility: to whom will it be useful!
section.title: Purpose of data

selectData.label: Select the data type from the list

labelData.label: If you chose option "Others", please specify type here

fieldSet.title: Type of data generated/collected
fieldSet.description:
Type of data depends on purpose and collection/generation methods. 
Dataset may consist of both primary and secondary data in some cases, however, secondary data is more re-usable as it has undergone processing and cleaning.

selectData.label: Select data format from the list

labelData.label: If you chose option "Others", please specify format here

fieldSet.title: Format of data generated/collected
fieldSet.description:
The choice of a preferred file format is crucial in order to ensure that your data will be readable also in the future. Some file formats are more likely to allow long-term readability than others are. Such formats are usually:
 - non-proprietary
 - open, with documented international standards
 - using standard character encoding, preferably Unicode (e.g. UTF-8)
 - uncompressed (space permitting)

labelData.label: Numerical value

fieldSet.title: Expected volume of the data
fieldSet.description: Size of data is significant when it comes to calculating necessary storage space, as well as space in the designated repository. Here you are expected to give approximate byte size of the data

labelData.label: If No, please describe, if you have considered re-using any of existing data?

fieldSet.title: Are you re-using this dataset?
fieldSet.description:
Before collection of new data, researchers shall consider possibilities to re-use already prepared data within or outside their institution, thus saving human and financial resources.
If you are planning to use other researchers’ data in your research there are a couple of things that you need to take into consideration, such as copyright of datasets, permissions provided through licenses, ethical aspects for reuse, etc.

referenceTypeData.label: Indicate repository where the data set has been deposited

labelData.label: If repository isn't in the list, please indicate its title
If repository isn't in the list, please indicate its URL
Add persistent identifier of the data set

fieldSet.title: If Yes, please indicate what dataset are you re-using?
fieldSet.description: In this case you have to fill rest of the DMP according to characteristics of the data you are re-using, as well as your plans on documenting and processing the data. If the data is stored in repositories such as Zenodo, you can auto-fill description of this dataset.

section.description: Here you are expected to provide the basic information on types, formats and size of data generated/collected in this dataset. It is important to specify, if you will use already existing dataset (re-using data).
section.title: Data characteristics

selectData.label: Please select the used Data Identifier System(s)

labelData.label: If you chose other, please specify the respective standard identification mechanism here

fieldSet.title: Will data be attributed with standard identification mechanism (e.g. persistent and unique identifiers such as Digital Object Identifiers – DOI)?
fieldSet.description: In case of RSU Dataverse, DOI is automatically allocated upon publishing of the dataset

labelData.label: If you chose option "No", please explain why you don't plan to use any metadata

fieldSet.title: Do you plan to provide metadata for your data?
fieldSet.description: Metadata is a crucial part of data publishing, ensuring discoverability of your dataset

referenceTypeData.label: Select metadata standard from the list

labelData.label: If it's not on the list, please specify the title here
If it's not on the list, please specify URL here
If there are no standards in your discipline, describe what type of metadata will be created and how.

uploadData.label: If desired, upload an image of the used schema

fieldSet.title: Will you use any metadata standard?
fieldSet.description: RSU Dataverse applies DDI metadata standard. In cases of extensive metadata and specific schemes, it is possible also to attach file (for example, .txt) with complementary metadata

labelData.label: If you will use any convention or controlled vocabulary, specify title here
If you will use any convention or controlled vocabulary, specify URL here

fieldSet.title: Will search keywords be provided that optimize possibilities for re-use?
fieldSet.description: You shall provide descriptive keywords regarding the dataset you are publishing - they can highlight both the contents of the dataset and also the potential applications.

fieldSet.title: Will you provide clear version numbers?
fieldSet.description: RSU Dataverse provides automatic versioning of the dataset.

selectData.label: What type of documentation do you plan to provide?

labelData.label: if you chose "Other", please specify type of documentation here

fieldSet.title: Will you document the dataset?
fieldSet.description: Documenting the dataset is very significant to ensure possibilities for researchers (including you on a later stage) to re-use the dataset.

fieldSet.title: Other relevant remarks
fieldSet.description: In case if dataset consists of many files and has a complex structure, it would be relevant to describe your approach to file organization (including structure of files, file naming etc.)

section.description: In this section you have to explain provisions for description and documentation of your data in order to make them findable and re-usable. For that you have to outline the discoverability of data (metadata provision) and use of standard identification mechanisms (persistent and unique identifiers). You have to specify, if you plan to use any naming conventions for keywords. Will it be possible to clearly see versions of the dataset? Also very important to specify standards used for metadata creation. If there are no standards in your discipline, describe what type of metadata will be created and how. Last but not least, you have to decide what kind of documentation you will provide to your data.
section.title: Metadata and documentation

fieldSet.title: Are the data formats open to software applications and to recombination with different data sets?
fieldSet.description: Please describe what are the expected formats (according to Data Summary) and are they accessible with widely used and/or free software?

labelData.label: If there is a specific software tool needed, specify it's title here
If there is a specific software tool needed, specify it's version here
If there is a specific software tool needed, specify source's URL here

fieldSet.title: What methods or software tools are needed to access the data?
Is it possible to include the relevant software in the dataset (e.g. source code)?
fieldSet.description: It is important to include source code in cases when researchers are employing very specific or self-made software to collect/generate or process the data. If possible, the source could be deposited also in repositories specifically developed for that (for example, GitHub).

referenceTypeData.label: If yes, please choose from the list

labelData.label: If not on the list, please specify title here
If not on the list, please specify URL here

Describe your choice and how it will make your data understandable and re-usable for other researchers. Will you be using standard vocabulary/taxonomy/ontology for all data types present in your data set, to allow inter-disciplinary interoperability? Describe them! In case it is unavoidable that you use uncommon or generate project specific ontologies or vocabularies, will you provide mappings to more commonly used ontologies?

fieldSet.title: Will you use data standard vocabulary/taxonomy/ontology to make your data interoperable?
Other relevant remarks

section.description: In this section you have to describe how you will handle your data in order to make them interoperable and re-usable.  To make data more accessible and re-usable, you also have to describe how the data will be made available by specifying methods and software tools needed to access and use the data. Significant part is to explain how you will describe the data, will you use any standard vocabulary/taxonomy/ontology.
section.title: Data accessability

referenceTypeData.label: Please choose repository from the list

labelData.label: If your repository is not on the list, please specify it's title here
If your repository is not on the list, please specify it's URL here
Please justify your choice! Have you explored appropriate arrangements with the identified repository?

fieldSet.title: Will data, associated metadata, documentation and code be made accessible with means of a repository?
fieldSet.description: Preference should be given to certified repositories which support open access where possible. In RSU case data shall be deposited in RSU Dataverse, if there are no specific requirements from the funder or other restrictions to size of the dataset or type of dataset

referenceTypeData.label: Please indicate the type of licence you are planning to use

labelData.label: Shortly explain your choice

fieldSet.title: How will the data be licensed to permit the widest re-use possible? Indicate the license planned to use
fieldSet.description: Data in RSU Dataverse can be licences with one of the copyrights licences provided by Creative Commons - https://creativecommons.org/licenses/

visibilityRule.value: open (anyone is able to access data without restrictions)
restricted, request access (access is restricted, but request with collaboration proposal could be submitted to the authors)
restricted, no access (access is denied, files will be opened only through contact person to the authors)

options.label: open (anyone is able to access data without restrictions)
options.value: open (anyone is able to access data without restrictions)

options.label: restricted, request access (access is restricted, but request with collaboration proposal could be submitted to the authors)
options.value: restricted, request access (access is restricted, but request with collaboration proposal could be submitted to the authors)

options.label: restricted, no access (access is denied, files will be opened only through contact person to the authors)
options.value: restricted, no access (access is denied, files will be opened only through contact person to the authors)

labelData.label: If there are restrictions on use, how will access be provided? If certain datasets cannot be shared or need to be shared under restrictions, explain why. Clearly separate legal and contractual reasons from voluntary restrictions. Note that in multi-beneficiary projects it is also possible for specific beneficiaries to keep their data closed if relevant provisions are made in the consortium agreement and are in line with the reasons for opting out

fieldSet.title: What type of access the dataset will have (choose one)?
fieldSet.description: It is important to note that data shall be as open as possible, as closed as necessary, i.e. data or at least parts of the data shall be open to everyone. If there are any specific reasons to restrict access, these should be clearly listed

labelData.label: Please indicate here the last data of embargo period
Please specify the embargo conditions here

fieldSet.title: Will there be an embargo period for access to these data?
fieldSet.description: Embargo period shall be applied only in cases when there are certain research outputs based on this dataset to be published. Thus embargo period shall not exceed time appropriate for publishing the respective research outputs.

labelData.label: If possible, indicate approximate date for that
How long data will remain re-usable for others?

fieldSet.title: When data will be made available for re-use?
fieldSet.description: Here you should specify plans for the date of publishing the dataset in repository (with or without embargo period).

fieldSet.title: Will you provide reference to the dataset in scientific publications and other research outputs?
fieldSet.description: It is significant to provide link to the dataset, as well as data availability statement (if applicable) in research outputs such as scientific publications. In more and more journals it is already mandatory to provide a link to dataset in a trusted repository. It also gives more validity and trustworthiness to the publication. Otherwise, it is possible that public dataset will gain new collaborations

fieldSet.title: Other relevant remarks

section.description: In this section you have to describe plans for publishing this dataset (including information on repository, license), as well as what will be the access to dataset and how it will be accessible to third parties.
section.title: Data publishing and long-term preservation

labelData.label: If you chose option "Other", please indicate it here
Numerical value

fieldSet.title: Which costs will be covered within this project for making data FAIR?
Have you discussed how the costs will be covered?
fieldSet.description: Will there be costs covered partly also from other sources of funding? Note that you have to check your grant agreement. In RSU Dataverse long term preservation costs are covered by RSU.

labelData.label: Shortly describe distribution of tasks between the institutions

fieldSet.title: Which institutions are responsible for handling the data? Which institution(s) are the data controller in this project?
fieldSet.description: Here, it is necessary to indicate all institutions that will process the data, specifying the specific institution that determines the purposes of data processing and the means by which the data will be processed, and which will therefore be responsible for data security—the data controller. There may be several data controllers if several data sets are created in different institutions and only combined at the final stage. In cases where the same data is processed in several institutions at different stages of a project, there may be several controllers, unless otherwise specified in the cooperation agreement.

labelData.label: Shortly describe distribution of tasks related to data managament process

fieldSet.title: Who will be responsible for data management in your project? Which person will be the data manager responsible for the correct processing of data in the project, staff training, and maintenance of the data management plan?
Who will collect and process the data for this study (name data processors- researchers, laboratory technicians, assistants, and other persons who will work with the data for this study)?

labelData.label: Explain data quality assurance processes, also after the end of the project, in order to keep data updated and properly available for re-use

fieldSet.title: Are there data quality assurance processes foreseen?

section.description: In this section you have to outline the resources available to create, publish and store your data. Therefore, estimate the costs for making your data FAIR. Describe how you intend to cover these costs. Clearly identify responsibilities for data management in your project and describe quality assurance procedures to keep your data up to date.
section.title: Allocation of resources and quality assurance

labelData.label: If you chose option "Other", please specify here
Describe these data security measures - how are you going to apply them

fieldSet.title: What security measures will be used for data security?

labelData.label: If you chose option "Other", please specify here
Describe these data security provisions, taking into account these shall be applied also before publishing data

fieldSet.title: What provisions are in place for data security?
fieldSet.description: Please describe how you will ensure safety of the data during the research (including raw data).

labelData.label: Describe storage facility/software you will use to storage and analyse data during research. If it is outside the scope of your institution, you shall describe the arrangements with other institutions/facilities regarding data safety. Describe in detail the location and duration of data storage for both raw and processed data. For example, raw data will be deleted no later than ... months/years after processing is completed or last used, unless regulatory acts require longer storage. Processed data will be deleted or archived after the purpose of its use has expired (no later than ... years after publication or last active use).

fieldSet.title: Where the data will be storaged during research? For how long the data will be stored there?
fieldSet.description: When planning data management, it is crucial to understand how and where data will be storaged, including phases where data is analyzed or pseudonymized. Describe the lenght of raw and processed data storage.

fieldSet.title: When and how will data from the specified storage location be deleted or destroyed?
fieldSet.description:
Deletion method:
Indicate whether the data will be deleted or destroyed using an electronic deletion method. 
Electronic files will be permanently deleted using secure data overwriting or encryption deletion methods (e.g., DoD 5220.22-M or equivalent).
Physical media and paper documents will be destroyed mechanically (shredding, burning) or handed over to a certified data destruction service provider.
Documentation:
For sensitive data - a report must be prepared for the deletion, specifying the type of data, the deletion method, the date, and the person responsible.
The deletion need to be confirmed and verified by IT specialist to ensure that the data cannot be recovered.

labelData.label: If yes, describe how the respective repository will ensure long term preservation and curation

fieldSet.title: Have you ensured that your chosen repository is appropriate for long term preservation and curation?
fieldSet.description: RSU Dataverse has all the necessary measures to ensure secure long term preservation and curation

section.description: In this section you have to delineate the provisions and measures to ensure security of your data both during the project and after it.
section.title: Data security

labelData.label: If yes, please describe them here

fieldSet.title: Are there any ethical or legal issues that could have an impact on data collection and sharing?
fieldSet.description: Ethical and legal issues are mostly related to personal data and sensitive information, but it certainly isn't limited to it. You have to consider also commercial aspects, contractual arrangements with the funder, possibility of classified information etc.

fieldSet.title: Have you got/will you get permission from ethics committee to collect and process data (if applicable)?
Is it planned to conduct an informed consent process for research subjects?
Where and for how long will the signed informed consent forms be stored? Who will have access to these documents?
Is informed consent for data sharing and long-term preservation included in questionnaires dealing with personal data (if applicable)?
fieldSet.description: If it is planned to openly share pseudonimized sensitive data via repository an informed consent should be exclusively asked to share this data in this particular way.

fieldSet.title: Will data collected/generated include personal data or any other sensitive information?
fieldSet.description: Examples of personal data and sensitive information include- contact information of research participants, health data, data about ones political and religious views, location data etc.

fieldSet.title: Is the collected/generated data intended to be processed outside the EU?
fieldSet.description: If the collected/generated data is to be processed outside the EU, please indicate the country and institution where this is to be done, as well as the RSU data protection specialist (DPS) with whom you will consult before transferring the data for processing.

fieldSet.title: Is the collected/generated personal data intended to be used for profiling and/or automated decision-making?
fieldSet.description:
If the collected/generated personal data is intended to be used for profiling* and/or automated decision-making**, please indicate the RSU DPS that you will consult before using the data for this purpose.
*Profiling involves categorizing individuals based on their characteristics. According to the GDPR, the controller should provide the data subject with any additional information necessary to ensure fair and transparent processing, taking into account the specific circumstances and context in which the personal data are processed. In addition, the data subject should be informed about profiling and the consequences of such profiling.
**Automated decision-making and profiling based on special categories of personal data should only be allowed under specific conditions. Technical and organizational requirements must be met.

fieldSet.title: Is artificial intelligence intended to be used in the processing of collected/generated sensitive or personal data?
fieldSet.description: If artificial intelligence is to be used in the processing of collected/generated sensitive or personal data, indicate the RSU DPS and IT specialist you will consult before using the data for this purpose.

options.label: Data accompanied by informed consent statements
options.value: data accompanied by informed consent statements

labelData.label: If you chose option "Other", please specify here
If data set will include personal data/sensitive information, describe methods for processing and accessing data (including anonymization/pseudonymisation of data)

fieldSet.title: What methods do you intend to use to process and access the data securely?

section.description: In this section you have to describe any ethical or legal issues related to data you plan to publish. You especially have to consider how you prevent access to personal data or that you protect sensitive information. You also have to describe technical methods to process and access personal data or sensitive information. In order to fill this section, it is also significant to review national legislation, as well as contact ethics committee. In some cases there could be legal aspects to consider regarding licensed data sources.
section.title: Ethical and legal aspects

field.textValue: The informed consent process includes permission for the use of pseudonymized research data for scientific analysis, publication, and long-term preservation. Participants were informed that their biological samples and derived data may be used for research purposes and that only pseudonymized data may be shared in trusted research repositories. Scientific publications may present results either in aggregated form or at the level of individual samples using study-specific pseudonymized identifiers. These identifiers cannot be linked to identifiable individuals without access to the secure key held by the research team, ensuring that individual participants cannot be identified. Raw sequencing reads may be deposited in recognized genomic repositories such as the European Nucleotide Archive (ENA) where appropriate ethical approvals and pseudonymization procedures are ensured. Only pseudonymized genomic data without direct personal identifiers are shared. Processed analytical outputs are deposited in the RSU Dataverse repository.
Standard scientific terminology used in genomics and reproductive biology literature.

Standardized vocabularies and taxonomies will be used to ensure interoperability and reusability of the dataset. Microbial classification results are based on the NCBI Taxonomy, which provides globally recognized taxonomic identifiers and hierarchical structure. Metadata describing samples, sequencing runs, and analytical outputs will follow commonly used terminology in genomics and bioinformatics and will be documented in accompanying metadata files and a README file. The use of standard identifiers and controlled vocabularies facilitates integration with other genomic datasets and supports reuse by researchers across different disciplines.

The dataset will be deposited in the institutional repository and made publicly available after the embargo period ends on 31 March 2028. The embargo allows time for publication of the primary research results and completion of ongoing intellectual property procedures. After this date, the dataset will be openly accessible with accompanying metadata and documentation to facilitate reuse by the research community.

Yes. The study involves analysis of human-derived biological material and genomic sequencing data, which may contain sensitive information. All samples are processed in accordance with approved ethical protocols and institutional regulations governing research involving human biological material. Personal identifiers are removed or replaced with pseudonymized identifiers prior to data processing to ensure participant confidentiality. In addition, certain analytical methods developed within the project may be subject to intellectual property protection, which may influence the extent to which some technical details or intermediate analysis components are publicly shared. Data sharing will therefore follow applicable ethical approvals, institutional data protection policies, and intellectual property considerations.

Research data are processed within secure institutional infrastructure at Rīga Stradiņš University and on the RTU High-Performance Computing (HPC) environment used for genomic data analysis. Access to the data is restricted to authorized project researchers and protected through institutional authentication and password-based access control. Computational analyses are performed on RTU high-performance computing systems with controlled user access. Data backups and storage security are maintained according to RSU institutional data management and IT security policies.

Research data are processed and stored within secure institutional infrastructure at Rīga Stradiņš University. Access to the data is restricted to authorized project personnel through authenticated institutional user accounts and role-based access controls. Sequencing data used for analysis are pseudonymized prior to computational processing. Data transfer between systems occurs through secure institutional networks, and processing is performed on protected servers within the RTU high-performance computing environment. Only authorized researchers involved in the project can access the datasets required for analysis.

textListValues.textListValue: data accompanied by informed consent statements

field.textValue: FASTQ, BAM (raw sequencing data stored on HPC and deposited in ENA)

Computational analyses in this project involve bioinformatic and statistical methods applied to pseudonymized sequencing data. No artificial intelligence systems are used to process identifiable personal data. The analyses focus on molecular features derived from sequencing data and are conducted for research purposes within secure institutional infrastructure.

Although the source code of the analytical pipeline will remain private during the patent protection phase, the deposited datasets will include sufficient methodological documentation, parameter descriptions, and derived outputs to allow independent verification of the reported results.

The study involves the research analysis of pseudonymized biological data to explore molecular signatures associated with spermatogenic activity. Although analytical models may identify patterns that could potentially support future clinical interpretation, the results are used for research purposes only and do not involve automated decision-making or profiling that produces decisions affecting individual participants. The study does not generate clinically actionable results for participants. Any potential clinical interpretation would require independent evaluation by qualified clinicians and would occur outside the scope of this research project.

Research data are stored within secure institutional infrastructures used by the project, including the RTU High-Performance Computing environment for sequencing data processing and RSU institutional systems for documentation and curated datasets. Access to the data is restricted to authorized project researchers and controlled through institutional authentication systems. Data processing and analysis are conducted on RTU high-performance computing infrastructure with user-level access control. Data are stored on institutional servers with regular backup procedures and maintained according to RSU IT security and research data management policies. Any datasets containing sensitive information are handled in accordance with applicable data protection regulations and institutional ethical approvals.

Rīga Stradiņš University (RSU) is the primary institution responsible for handling and managing the project data and acts as the data controller. RSU determines the purposes and means of data processing and ensures compliance with institutional data management and data protection policies. Data processing and computational analysis are performed on the High-Performance Computing (HPC) infrastructure operated by Riga Technical University (RTU), which is routinely used by RSU researchers for large-scale genomic analyses. Data storage and long-term preservation will be provided through the RSU Dataverse repository. Project researchers affiliated with RSU are responsible for data generation, curation, and documentation.

The data generated in the SPERMFREE project aim to investigate molecular signatures of spermatogenic activity and inflammation in men with severe male infertility using cell-free DNA (cfDNA) derived from seminal plasma. The collected data support the development and validation of an integrated analytical workflow that combines multiple cfDNA-derived features, including fragmentation patterns, inferred gene expression signals, mitochondrial DNA coverage, and microbial signatures, to provide a non-invasive assessment of testicular function.

The primary data originate from seminal plasma samples collected from patients undergoing clinical evaluation for infertility in collaborating fertility clinics. From these samples, cfDNA will be extracted and sequenced using next-generation sequencing (NGS) technologies. Subsequent bioinformatic analyses will generate derived datasets describing genomic fragmentation profiles, sequence motif patterns, microbial taxonomic composition, and gene-level fragmentation metrics. These molecular data will be integrated with pseudonymized clinical parameters, such as spermogram results and patient characteristics, to develop and validate the SPERMFREE analytical framework.

This data generation directly supports the project objective of establishing a reproducible and interpretable molecular workflow for assessing spermatogenic activity and inflammation-related dysfunction in severe male infertility.

Currently, all data processing and storage are performed within EU-based institutional infrastructure at Rīga Stradiņš University. No data transfer or processing outside the EU is planned within the scope of the present project. If future international collaborations require data transfer outside the EU, appropriate institutional approvals and data protection procedures will be implemented in accordance with GDPR and institutional policies.

Research data stored on institutional servers and HPC infrastructure will be retained for the duration of the project and for at least 5–10 years after project completion in accordance with institutional research data management policies. Temporary intermediate analysis files that are no longer required may be periodically removed from internal storage systems. Datasets that support scientific publications will be deposited in the RSU Dataverse repository and preserved for long-term access and reuse. Such published datasets will remain available indefinitely in accordance with repository preservation policies.

textListValues.textListValue: restricted, request access (access is restricted, but request with collaboration proposal could be submitted to the authors)

field.textValue:
Access to the datasets generated in this project will follow two complementary access models depending on the data type.

Raw sequencing reads generated from seminal plasma cfDNA will be deposited in the European Nucleotide Archive (ENA), an international repository for nucleotide sequencing data. These data will be shared in accordance with repository policies for human genomic data and will include standard sequencing metadata describing samples and experimental conditions. No direct personal identifiers (such as participant names or clinical identifiers) will be included. Samples will be referenced only through study-specific pseudonymized identifiers.

Processed analytical datasets derived from sequencing data will be deposited in the RSU Dataverse institutional repository. These datasets include aggregated analytical results and tabular outputs necessary to reproduce the reported analyses. However, certain components of the analytical workflow, including elements of the bioinformatic pipeline and intermediate analysis structures, are part of a potentially patentable analytical technology developed within the SPERMFREE project. For this reason, access to some processed datasets and associated technical documentation may be restricted and provided upon reasonable request to the research team after evaluation of the request and in accordance with institutional intellectual property policies.

An embargo period may be applied until the primary research results based on this dataset are published. After publication, the dataset will be made openly available in the institutional repository with accompanying metadata and documentation. The embargo ensures that the research team has sufficient time to publish the main scientific findings while maintaining compliance with open science principles.

The dataset will be licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) license to allow the widest possible reuse while ensuring proper citation of the dataset by future users. This license permits sharing and adaptation of the data for research and educational purposes while maintaining attribution to the original authors.

Research data will be organized in a structured directory system separating raw sequencing data, intermediate analysis files, and processed datasets. Raw sequencing reads (FASTQ) and alignment files (BAM/CRAM) will be processed and stored within the RTU High Performance Computing infrastructure. Processed analytical datasets used for statistical analysis and publications will be stored in structured tabular formats (TSV/CSV).

File naming will follow consistent conventions including sample identifiers, processing stage, and file type (e.g., sampleID_fastq, sampleID_aligned.bam, sampleID_fragment_metrics.tsv). Processed datasets deposited in RSU Dataverse will include accompanying documentation files (README) describing dataset structure, variables, and analytical steps to facilitate interpretation and reuse.
250 GB

The project will use widely adopted open and non-proprietary data formats commonly used in genomics and bioinformatics. Raw sequencing data will be stored in FASTQ format, while aligned sequencing reads will be stored in BAM or CRAM formats. Processed analytical datasets will be stored in tabular formats such as TSV or CSV. These formats are widely supported by open-source bioinformatic tools and statistical software, including samtools, BWA, Kraken2, R, and Python-based analysis environments. The use of standardized formats ensures interoperability with other datasets and facilitates reuse and long-term accessibility.

The datasets generated in the SPERMFREE project may be useful to several groups in the future. First, they will support researchers working in reproductive biology, genomics, and molecular diagnostics who study the biological mechanisms of male infertility and spermatogenic failure. The processed datasets describing cfDNA fragmentation patterns, microbial signatures, and inferred gene expression profiles may contribute to methodological development in liquid biopsy research beyond oncology.

Second, clinicians and researchers in andrology and reproductive medicine may use these data to better understand molecular markers associated with spermatogenic activity and inflammation-related dysfunction, potentially improving diagnostic strategies for severe male infertility.

The datasets may also be relevant to bioinformaticians and computational biologists developing analytical approaches for fragmentomics and cfDNA-based diagnostics. In addition, aggregated and pseudonymized results may inform healthcare researchers and policy makers interested in improving diagnostic pathways for male infertility.

Finally, the data may support future translational and technological developments in the biotechnology and diagnostics sectors, including companies developing molecular diagnostic tools or liquid biopsy technologies.

Data quality assurance will be ensured through multiple quality control steps implemented during data generation and bioinformatic processing. Raw sequencing data quality will be evaluated using standard sequencing metrics and preprocessing tools such as fastp for read filtering and trimming. Alignment statistics, coverage metrics, and classification outputs will be examined to verify data integrity and consistency. Intermediate results will be inspected to identify potential technical artefacts, and datasets will be checked for completeness and consistency before deposition and further analysis.

Marija Lazovska is responsible for data management within the project. Responsibilities include organization and documentation of datasets, implementation of the data management plan, coordination of data processing workflows, and preparation of datasets and metadata for deposition in the RSU Dataverse repository. The data manager ensures that data are stored securely within RSU infrastructure, processed according to established bioinformatics workflows, and prepared in accordance with FAIR data principles.
fastp
https://github.com/OpenGene/fastp

The data will be accessible using widely used open-source bioinformatics and statistical software. Raw sequencing data in FASTQ format can be accessed and processed using standard genomic tools such as FastQC, BWA, and samtools. Alignment files in BAM or CRAM formats can be handled using samtools and related bioinformatics utilities. Processed tabular datasets (TSV/CSV) can be accessed using common data analysis software such as R, Python, or spreadsheet applications. Microbiome analysis outputs generated with Kraken2 and related tools can be accessed using standard bioinformatics workflows. These tools are freely available and widely used in the genomics research community, ensuring that the data can be accessed and reused without requiring proprietary software.

fieldSet.comment: Data collection and processing within this study will primarily be carried out by Marija Lazovska. Responsibilities include organization of research data, bioinformatic processing of sequencing datasets, integration of analytical results, and preparation of datasets and metadata for long-term storage and reuse. The work is conducted within the research project framework at Rīga Stradiņš University under the scientific supervision of Linda Gailite and Juris Ērenpreiss. All data processing activities follow institutional research data management and data protection policies.

field.textValue: Signed informed consent forms are stored securely at Rīga Stradiņš University in accordance with institutional ethical and data protection requirements. Paper consent forms are kept in locked cabinets within restricted-access research facilities, while any electronic records are stored on secure institutional systems with controlled access. Access to the consent documentation is limited to authorized members of the research team and the principal investigator, and may be provided to authorized institutional auditors or ethics committees if required. Consent forms will be retained for at least 10 years after completion of the study in accordance with institutional and ethical regulations.

The costs related to making data FAIR are minimal and are covered primarily by existing institutional infrastructure. Data storage and long-term preservation will be provided through the RSU Dataverse repository, where archiving costs are covered by RSU. Computational analyses are performed using RTU high-performance computing infrastructure. Data curation, metadata preparation, and dataset documentation will be carried out by project researchers as part of their regular research activities funded by the project. Therefore, no additional external funding sources are required specifically for FAIR data management.

The SPERMFREE project primarily generates original data through sequencing and analysis of seminal plasma cell-free DNA samples collected within the study. The dataset will consist of newly generated genomic sequencing data and derived bioinformatic results produced during the project. Therefore, external datasets produced by other researchers are not reused as a primary data source.

However, publicly available reference resources such as the human reference genome and standard bioinformatic databases (e.g., genomic annotations and microbial reference databases used in taxonomic classification) will be used as part of the analytical workflow. These resources are widely used scientific reference datasets and do not constitute reuse of research datasets.

During the research project, raw and processed sequencing data will be stored and processed on the RTU High-Performance Computing (HPC) infrastructure, which provides secure storage, controlled user access, and computational resources for genomic data analysis. Data are stored on institutional HPC servers with controlled user access and regular backup procedures. Raw sequencing data and intermediate analysis outputs will be retained on the HPC storage systems throughout the duration of the project to support ongoing analyses and verification of results. After completion of the project and publication of the main research outputs, relevant datasets and associated metadata will be deposited in the RSU Dataverse repository for long-term preservation and reuse. Raw and processed research data will be retained within institutional storage systems for at least 5–10 years in accordance with institutional research data management policies.

During the project, raw sequencing reads are archived in international nucleotide repositories such as the European Nucleotide Archive (ENA), while the RSU Dataverse repository stores processed analytical datasets and associated metadata required to reproduce the reported analyses. Due to the large size and technical nature of raw sequencing files (e.g., FASTQ or BAM), the Dataverse repository will primarily contain processed analysis outputs and derived datasets (e.g., tabular result files and associated metadata). These datasets will be accompanied by structured metadata and persistent identifiers to ensure long-term accessibility, citation, and reuse. The repository is maintained within RSU institutional infrastructure and provides secure long-term data preservation and curation.
